planting.note: Plant grafted or established trees in spring in a sheltered site.

directions: A prized large-fruited selection that bears younger than common seedlings. Keep the soil acidic, moist, and well-mulched, and avoid letting the roots dry out. Harvest when the big fruit turns glossy black and softens; the thick skin encloses sweet, aromatic pulp best enjoyed fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil kept moist.

directions: This jaboticaba bears reddish-purple to dark fruit directly on the woody stems. Give it acidic, humus-rich soil and steady moisture, and mulch to keep roots cool. It is slow but productive with age. Harvest when fruit colors fully and softens; enjoy fresh or made into jelly and juice.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; a large-fruited red selection.

directions: A well-known heavy-cropping red cultivar with large, deep-red fruit. Start seed warm, pot on, and set out in fertile, sheltered ground after the last frost. Stake against wind and keep evenly watered while fruit sizes. Harvest when the skin is glossy red and the fruit softens; the flesh is rich and tangy.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost; a sweeter gold-fruited selection.

directions: A golden-fruited tamarillo often milder and sweeter than the red types. Sow seed in warmth, grow on, and plant into rich, sheltered soil once frost has passed. Support the brittle stems and mulch to hold moisture. Harvest when the fruit ripens to a clear amber-gold and gives to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; a productive, reliably fruiting selection.

directions: A dependable cutting-grown selection that sets fruit well even without much heat. Plant in rich soil, cage or stake the lax stems, and keep moisture steady while fruit develops. Feed lightly for good yields. Harvest when the pale fruit shows purple striping and yields slightly, with a sweet, mild melon flavor.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in full sun.

directions: A large-fruited pepino selection grown from cuttings for uniform results. Set plants in well-drained, fertile soil and support the trailing growth off the ground. Water evenly and mulch to keep fruit clean. Harvest when the skin turns golden with purple stripes and the fruit softens slightly, releasing a sweet melon aroma.

planting.note: Plant after last frost in a warm, sunny location.

directions: Give this variety full sun and lean, fast-draining soil for the best flavor, which many consider among the finest of any pomegranate. Keep pruning light and mostly to shape and to thin crowded growth. The red arils have small, soft, edible seeds and sweeten fully by mid autumn.

planting.note: One of the hardier types; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Choose this Russian selection where winters run colder than most pomegranates tolerate, and mulch the base heavily in its first few years. It fruits reliably and vigorously once settled, so allow room for a large bush or small multi-trunk tree. Large fruit with pink-red arils ripens in autumn and holds well on the plant.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun; tolerates coastal conditions.

directions: Grow in full sun where its very sweet, nearly seedless arils and clear juice can develop with plenty of heat. It stays a bit more compact than some, so it suits smaller yards or large containers. Fruit ripens over a long window from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a hot, sunny spot.

directions: Plant in full sun and allow room, as this variety is known for producing unusually large fruit. Water deeply during fruit set and swell, then ease off as ripening nears to concentrate flavor. Pale pink arils with a sweet-tart taste are ready in autumn.

planting.note: Fine for containers; plant out after frost.

directions: Grow this compact form in a pot or as a low hedge, as it blooms and fruits while quite small and young. It produces many showy orange-red flowers followed by small ornamental fruit that is edible but seedy. Give full sun and keep it lightly pruned to maintain a tidy, rounded shape.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; handle the taproot carefully.

directions: Cold-stratify seed for several weeks, then sow in deep pots since young trees resent root disturbance. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and stake young trees against wind. Fruit colors up in autumn, and astringent types must soften to jelly-ripe before eating while non-astringent types can be eaten firm.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep soil and water evenly during the growing season for large fruit. This astringent variety produces big, elongated, pointed fruit prized for drying into hoshigaki. Allow the fruit to become jelly-soft before eating fresh, harvesting in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees dormant in early spring; naturally compact.

directions: This naturally smaller, earlier-ripening tree suits tighter spaces and still wants full sun. The non-astringent fruit can be eaten firm once it colors up, and it matures earlier in the season than most kaki. Keep young trees evenly watered and lightly pruned to build a strong framework.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; self-fruitful.

directions: Plant this self-fertile, cold-hardy selection in full sun where a single tree can set fruit without a pollinator. Give it room, as it grows into a substantial shade tree over time. The seedless-to-few-seeded orange fruit ripens in autumn and is sweetest once fully soft.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; a female needing a pollinator for best set.

directions: Grow this large-fruited variety in full sun, ideally with a male American persimmon nearby to improve set. Young trees establish best with even moisture and a mulch ring to reduce competition. The comparatively large orange fruit ripens in autumn and must be fully soft before eating.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: This heavy-bearing selection, also sold as Juhl, does well in full sun and warm summers. It produces abundant, flavorful orange fruit and benefits from a nearby male tree for reliable crops. Harvest in autumn once the fruit is completely soft and sweet.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant in full sun where its early ripening lets fruit finish before the coldest weather. It sometimes bears a few perfect flowers and can set some fruit alone, but crops improve with a pollinator. The sweet, golden-orange fruit ripens in early to mid autumn and should be soft before eating.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; hardier than pure kaki.

directions: This hybrid blends Asian fruit quality with American cold tolerance, so it suits gardens a bit too cold for straight kaki. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and give it a sheltered spot to protect early growth from late frosts. The medium, astringent fruit ripens late in autumn and is eaten once fully soft.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; notably cold-hardy hybrid.

directions: Grow this hardy Ukrainian hybrid in full sun where winters are too harsh for Japanese persimmon alone. It is vigorous and often used as both a fruiting tree and cold-hardy rootstock. The astringent orange fruit ripens in mid to late autumn and needs to soften fully before eating.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; extremely drought and heat tolerant once set.

directions: Seed is slow and variable, so most gardeners plant grafted trees, but seedlings and root suckers grow readily in hot sites. Give full sun and any well-drained soil, and expect strong tolerance of drought, heat, and alkaline ground. The small fruit is crisp and apple-like when green-brown and turns wrinkled and date-like as it dries on the tree in autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; pairs well with Li.

directions: Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and pair it with another variety such as Li to boost fruiting. It produces elongated, pear-shaped fruit that is best eaten after it has partly dried and reddened on the tree. Harvest through autumn as fruit shifts from crisp to sweet and date-like.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: This small-fruited variety is prized for exceptionally sweet, juicy, crisp fruit despite its modest size. Give it full sun and dry, well-drained soil, and expect reliable heavy crops even in young trees. The little round fruit is best eaten fresh at the reddish-brown stage in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; can be thorny.

directions: Plant in full sun in hot, dry conditions, and be aware this variety tends to have prominent thorns. It ripens early and bears very sweet, crunchy round fruit that many eat straight off the tree. Harvest as fruit turns brown in late summer, using care around the spines.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: This variety is noted for producing some of the largest jujube fruit, so give it full sun and room to size up. Water to establish, then treat it as drought-tolerant in well-drained ground. The big, round-to-oval fruit is eaten fresh when crisp or left to wrinkle and sweeten in autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees after frost; shade young trees at first.

directions: An old, well-regarded variety that produces large fruit with a rich flavor and few seeds per cluster. Plant with a different pawpaw for pollination and keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist. Harvest in autumn when the fruit softens and its skin lightens slightly.

planting.note: Set young trees out in spring once hard frosts have passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in fast-draining soil with the root ball level to the surrounding grade, keeping the crown dry. Water deeply but infrequently once established, as standing moisture invites root rot. Fruit ripens through autumn from green to black; pick green for firmer olives or wait for full color for oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost; this compact, early-bearing Spanish cultivar suits containers.

directions: Site in full sun with sharp drainage and plant at the same depth it grew in the nursery pot. This self-fertile variety fruits young and heavily, so thin lightly to keep branches from breaking. Harvest the small dark drupes in mid to late autumn for a mild, buttery oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact Catalan oil variety often paired with Arbequina in hedgerows.

directions: Plant into gritty, well-drained ground and avoid burying the trunk flare. Its restrained, weeping habit takes well to high-density planting and light pruning. Pick the small fruit in late autumn as it darkens for a peppery, aromatic oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this vigorous Greek oil cultivar is prized for high yields of tiny fruit.

directions: Give it a hot, dry site with excellent drainage and keep the graft or crown above soil level. The small fruit ripens unevenly, so harvest over several weeks as drupes color up. Its oil is intensely fruity and high in polyphenols, best pressed soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a widely grown Spanish table olive with a spreading, low crown.

directions: Set out in full sun on free-draining soil and stake young trees against wind. Prune to open the spreading canopy so light reaches the interior fruiting wood. Pick the plump rounded fruit green for classic table olives, or let it blacken for a richer cure.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this hardy California heirloom is among the more cold-tolerant olives.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil where the upright tree has room to reach its full height. It tolerates cold better than most, but shelter it from prolonged freezes. Harvest for oil or table use as fruit turns from green to glossy black in late autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this classic Tuscan oil cultivar is a reliable, partly self-fertile pollinizer.

directions: Choose a warm, sheltered site with lean, well-drained soil and plant at nursery depth. Its slightly pendulous branches respond well to light annual thinning. Pick fruit as it turns purple-black for a green, peppery oil that defines Tuscan blends.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a vigorous Italian oil variety noted for early, uniform ripening and good cold tolerance.

directions: Plant in full sun on ground that never stays wet, keeping the crown clear of mulch. It is not fully self-fertile, so pair with Frantoio or Pendolino for best set. The fruit blackens early and evenly, making a mild, well-balanced oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this French cultivar yields the slender green olives sold as Picholine.

directions: Site in full sun with sharp drainage and protect from cold winter winds. Prune to keep an open vase so the elongated fruit colors evenly. Harvest green and firm for the traditional crisp, salty table cure.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this famous Greek table olive bears large, almond-shaped black fruit.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil in full sun and stake until the trunk thickens. It benefits from a compatible pollinizer nearby to set a full crop. Let the large fruit ripen to deep purple-black before harvesting for the classic wine-and-vinegar cure.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a large-fruited Spanish table olive often used for green cocktail olives.

directions: Give it full sun and roomy, well-drained soil to support its heavy, oversized fruit. The tree is a shy oil producer, so grow it strictly for the table. Harvest the big green drupes before they soften for firm, meaty cured olives.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this Italian table variety produces large, tender-fleshed green olives.

directions: Plant in full sun on light, well-drained soil and handle its brittle branches gently. The delicate fruit bruises easily, so pick by hand as it reaches full green size. Cure promptly after harvest to preserve the mild, sweet flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this weeping Italian variety is a valued universal pollinizer for orchards.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and expect a graceful, cascading habit. Grown mainly as a pollinizer, a single tree lifts fruit set across nearby oil cultivars. It also yields small drupes suitable for a light, fruity oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Tuscan oil variety often used to pollinate Frantoio and Leccino.

directions: Set into warm, well-drained soil in full sun and shelter from hard frosts. Its slightly weeping branches carry abundant small fruit that ripens mid-season. Press soon after harvest for a delicate, aromatic oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this southern Italian oil cultivar is known for robust, bitter-pungent oil.

directions: Plant in full sun on lean, fast-draining ground and prune to keep the canopy open. Largely self-fertile, it sets reliable crops even as a solo tree. Harvest as fruit turns violet for a green, intensely peppery, long-keeping oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this Sicilian variety yields the bright-green Castelvetrano table olive.

directions: Plant in a warm, sunny spot with excellent drainage and steady summer warmth. The rounded fruit is dual-purpose but prized picked green for the crisp, buttery Castelvetrano cure. Harvest before the drupes darken to keep their vivid color and sweet flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this modern Israeli cultivar bears early and heavily in warm climates.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil and water sparingly once rooted in. This vigorous, quick-bearing tree suits hot regions and produces both oil and table fruit. Harvest as the elongated drupes turn purple for a fruity oil, or earlier for green olives.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the dominant Spanish oil cultivar, valued for stable, high-yielding oil.

directions: Plant in full sun on deep, well-drained soil where it tolerates heat and some cold. Self-fertile and productive, it fruits reliably without a partner tree. Pick as the drupes color for a stable, slightly bitter oil rich in antioxidants.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Spanish variety grown both for oil and as a black table olive.

directions: Site in full sun with good drainage and tolerate a range of soils. The tree carries pale-backed leaves that shimmer in the wind and a heavy crop of dual-purpose fruit. Harvest black-ripe for table curing or press mid-ripe for a fresh, grassy oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Spanish variety of the Ebro valley used for black olives and mild oil.

directions: Plant in full sun on dry, well-drained soil suited to its Mediterranean origins. Prune lightly to maintain an open frame and steady bearing. Let the fruit ripen fully to black for both a sweet table olive and a delicate golden oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this Ligurian variety yields small fruit for delicate oil and table use.

directions: Give it a sheltered, sunny slope with sharp drainage as it prefers coastal warmth. The tree bears small drupes that ripen to brown-black over a long window. Harvest for a mild, sweet oil or brine-cure the little olives whole.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a central-Italian oil cultivar central to Umbrian and Tuscan blends.

directions: Plant in full sun on hillside soil with fast drainage and steady air movement. Its upright, compact habit suits closer spacing but benefits from a nearby pollinizer. Pick as the round fruit turns dark for a green, grassy, sharply peppery oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a central-Spanish oil cultivar named for its curved, horn-like fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun on dry, well-drained soil where it withstands heat, drought, and cold. The distinctive curved drupes ripen late and cling well to the branch. Press soon after harvest for a stable, aromatic oil with a bittersweet finish.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an Italian-derived oil variety long grown in California orchards.

directions: Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and give the vigorous tree room to spread. It bears heavily, so thinning improves fruit size and reduces alternate bearing. Harvest as the drupes darken for a mild, plentiful oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the leading Tunisian oil cultivar, well adapted to hot, arid ground.

directions: Site in full sun on dry, well-drained soil where it shrugs off heat and drought. Its spreading canopy carries small fruit that ripens through late autumn. Press promptly for a light, sweet oil with a soft, mellow character.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an ancient Levantine cultivar grown for high-polyphenol oil and table fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun on rocky, well-drained soil that mirrors its hillside homeland. Drought-tolerant and long-lived, it needs little water once established. Harvest green for firm table olives or ripe for a robust, peppery oil.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Greek table variety from the Amfissa region with rounded, ripe-picked fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil and shelter from cold winter winds. Allow the round fruit to ripen fully on the tree to deep purple-black. Cure the soft, ripe drupes gently to keep their mellow, fruity flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this Spanish variety produces some of the largest of all table olives.

directions: Plant in full sun on rich, well-drained soil to support the very large fruit. It sets erratically and needs good pollination, so grow companions nearby. Harvest the big green drupes firm for a meaty, mild table cure.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the dominant Moroccan cultivar grown for both table olives and oil.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil in a hot, dry setting and water sparingly. This vigorous, adaptable tree yields dependable dual-purpose crops. Pick green for table curing or let the fruit blacken for a fruity, everyday oil.

planting.note: Set out rooted offshoots in warm spring soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant a rooted offshoot with the base of the trunk at soil level in fast-draining, sandy ground and water deeply while it establishes. Only female palms fruit, so a pollinator male or hand-pollination is required, and heavy bunches should be thinned for larger fruit. Harvest the large, soft brown dates in autumn once they reach the fully wrinkled, sweet stage.

planting.note: Plant offshoots in late spring where summers are long and dry.

directions: Establish a rooted offshoot in deep, sandy soil and irrigate the root zone regularly despite the palm's drought hardiness. Provide a male palm or hand-pollinate the female flowers in spring, then thin the developing strands. Harvest the semi-dry, amber dates in mid to late autumn when firm and translucent.

planting.note: Set offshoots out in warm spring soil in a hot, dry-summer site.

directions: Plant a rooted offshoot in sandy, well-drained soil and keep the roots consistently watered through the heat. Hand-pollinate or provide a male palm, since only females bear fruit. Harvest Barhi at the crunchy yellow khalal stage or let it ripen fully to a soft, caramel-like brown in autumn.

planting.note: Plant offshoots after the last spring frost in a hot desert climate.

directions: Set a rooted offshoot into deep sandy soil and water steadily until well anchored, after which the palm tolerates considerable dryness. Pollinate female flowers by hand or with a nearby male palm each spring. Harvest the firm, semi-dry golden dates in autumn for a sweet, nutty flavor that stores well.

planting.note: Plant offshoots in spring; this is one of the shorter, earlier-ripening dates.

directions: Plant a rooted offshoot in sandy, well-drained soil and keep the roots watered as it establishes. Hand-pollinate female flowers in spring and thin the fruit strands for size. Harvest the soft, dark, richly sweet dates in early to mid autumn, earlier than most other cultivars.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy hybrid at its nursery depth and keep it watered the first year to establish strong roots. It is self-fertile and bears over a long season, so a single tree yields well. Harvest the nearly seedless, sweet-tart dark berries over several weeks in early to midsummer as they ripen almost black.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a warm, frost-protected location.

directions: Plant this warm-climate cultivar at nursery depth in a sheltered site and water regularly through establishment. It is prized for its extremely long, firm fruit and benefits from wind protection to avoid berry drop. Harvest the striking red-to-black berries, often two to four inches long, in late spring and early summer when fully dark and sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact enough for a large container.

directions: Plant this compact black mulberry in the ground or a roomy pot with rich, free-draining soil and water consistently. Its small stature makes it easy to net against birds and prune for shape. Harvest the large, intensely flavored dark-purple berries in summer as they soften and release from the branch.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant a grafted tree at nursery depth in well-drained soil; young trees are thorny and slow to fruit. Female trees set the best crops with a nearby male, though some bear parthenocarpic fruit. Harvest the round, red maroon fruit in autumn when soft and richly sweet, resembling a small mulberry-fig cross.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring with another cultivar nearby.

directions: Plant this selected cultivar at nursery depth and grow a second cornelian cherry nearby for reliable pollination. It was chosen for larger, pear-shaped fruit and comes into bearing sooner than seedlings. Harvest the reddish fruit in late summer when it softens and sweetens on the branch.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring alongside a second variety.

directions: Set this cultivar in well-drained soil at nursery depth and plant a companion cornelian cherry to boost fruit set. Selected for large, elongated red fruit, it flowers early and bears heavily once mature. Harvest in late summer when the fruit deepens in color and gives softly to the touch.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; upright habit and heavy early bloom.

directions: Plant this upright, free-flowering selection at nursery depth in full sun to partial shade. Its abundant early yellow bloom is ornamental, and a second cornelian cherry nearby improves cropping. Harvest the glossy red fruit in late summer once soft and tart-sweet.

planting.note: Plant this cold-hardy strain in early spring in colder walnut country.

directions: Site this hardy seed strain of Persian walnut in a spot sheltered from late spring frost, keeping the graft union above ground. Water deeply through the first summers while the taproot establishes. Nuts mature in early autumn and drop when the husks blacken and loosen.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; this is a late-leafing commercial cultivar.

directions: Set the grafted tree in deep, well-drained soil and provide a pollinizer for the fullest crops. Its late leaf-out helps it dodge spring frosts, and lateral-bearing wood gives heavy yields. Light-colored, high-quality kernels fill by early autumn when husks split cleanly.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted trees in early spring; very late to leaf out.

directions: Plant this old French cultivar where late spring frosts threaten, since it breaks bud very late. Keep young trees weeded and deeply watered through establishment. Elongated, well-sealed nuts ripen in mid autumn and are prized for reliable, clean kernels.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Set this named black walnut selection in deep soil with the graft union above grade and stake it the first season. It is grown for larger, easier-cracking nuts than wild seedlings. Nuts fill by early autumn and fall inside thick green husks that darken with ripeness.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; a hardy northern-adapted cultivar.

directions: Plant this hardy, scab-resistant cultivar suited to northern pecan country and cooler summers. It bears reliably and resists common leaf and nut diseases. Small to medium nuts with excellent kernels ripen in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this vigorous, upright cultivar that begins bearing at a young age. Give it a compatible pollinizer and manage scab where humidity is high. Medium to large nuts ripen in mid autumn as the husks open.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Set this productive cultivar with a type-I pollen shed alongside a later-shedding partner. It produces large nuts but needs good disease management in wet climates. Kernels fill by mid autumn when the four-valved husks split.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this disease-tolerant, well-adapted cultivar for dependable crops with modest spray needs. It bears consistently and holds up in humid regions. Small to medium nuts of good quality ripen in mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Grow this old cultivar known for very long, large nuts, giving it good soil and a pollinizer. Manage moisture and thinning so the big nuts fill their shells fully. Elongated kernels are ready by mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; a late-season, scab-resistant cultivar.

directions: Choose this scab-resistant cultivar for humid gardens where later ripening still fits the season. It carries good crops with lower disease pressure than many older sorts. Medium to large nuts mature in late autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this hardy, scab-tolerant cultivar prized for thick shells and good yields in wet climates. Its strong disease resistance suits low-spray home orchards. Large nuts with plump kernels ripen in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring; this is a pecan-hickory hybrid.

directions: Plant a grafted hican, the natural cross between pecan and hickory, in deep fertile soil with a pecan nearby for pollen. It blends hickory hardiness with pecan-like nut quality but can crop unevenly. Nuts ripen in mid to late autumn as the husks split.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant this well-known commercial cultivar in deep soil with the graft union above grade. Provide a compatible pollinizer for consistent set and manage blight in wet springs. Large, light, well-sealed nuts ripen in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or in autumn in mild regions.

directions: Plant this suckering shrub at the same depth it grew in the nursery and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool. Grow at least two genetically different plants nearby, since hazelnuts need cross-pollination from wind-borne catkins in late winter. Nuts drop in their husks in early autumn and are gathered from the ground once the shells turn tan.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in early spring.

directions: Train this vigorous cultivar to a single trunk or an open bush and remove suckers each spring. Pair it with a compatible pollinizer such as 'Butler' because it will not set a crop with its own pollen. Harvest the large round nuts in early fall as the husks loosen and the crop falls.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring while still leafless.

directions: This disease-resistant selection tolerates eastern filbert blight better than older cultivars, making it a durable orchard choice. Plant it with a recommended pollinizer and keep the shallow root zone weed-free and mulched. Gather the large kernels in early autumn once the husks split and nuts drop.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring.

directions: Prized for its very large, showy nuts, this cultivar needs a partner variety to pollinate well. Prune out crowded and low branches during winter dormancy to keep an open, productive frame. Nuts ripen in early to mid autumn and are collected from the ground after they fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Often planted as a pollinizer for 'Barcelona', this cultivar also yields good long nuts of its own. Give it full sun and well-drained soil and rub off basal suckers through the growing season. Collect the fallen nuts in early autumn when the husks open.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring.

directions: This compact, blight-resistant variety stays smaller than older cultivars and suits closer orchard spacing. Plant with a compatible pollinizer and mulch the root zone to conserve moisture. The medium kernels ripen and drop in early fall for easy ground harvest.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring.

directions: Grown as much for its deep purple leaves and catkins as for its nuts, this large filbert doubles as an ornamental screen. Plant in full sun to keep the foliage color strong and prune in late winter to shape. Purple-husked nuts ripen in early autumn where a compatible pollinizer is nearby.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: A long-lived orchard and timber tree, the sweet chestnut needs deep, lime-free soil and space to spread. Grow two compatible trees for pollination and keep the root zone free of standing water. Large, sweet nuts ripen in autumn and drop within their burs, which are collected and split to release the kernels.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: This European-Japanese hybrid is a leading orchard cultivar valued for its very large, easy-to-peel nuts. Plant a compatible pollinizer such as a Chinese chestnut nearby and keep the graft union above the soil line. Harvest the big glossy nuts as burs split in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted or seedling trees in early spring.

directions: This American-Chinese hybrid line was selected for blight resistance and reliable heavy crops in the southeastern United States. Plant two trees for cross-pollination in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch the young root zone. Sweet, medium-to-large nuts ripen and drop in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring while dormant.

directions: A vigorous French hybrid cultivar, this chestnut yields large, shiny, sweet nuts and shows good disease tolerance. Provide acidic, well-drained soil and a compatible pollinizer for a full crop. Nuts ripen in autumn and are gathered from split burs on the ground.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This is the dominant commercial almond, prized for its thin shell and smooth kernel. It is not self-fertile, so interplant a compatible pollinizer such as 'Carmel' or 'Mission' that blooms at the same time. Nuts are ready in late summer when hulls dry and split open.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: Also sold as 'Texas', this cultivar blooms slightly later and tolerates a bit more cold than most almonds. It serves as a good pollinizer for 'Nonpareil' and produces hard-shelled, richly flavored kernels. Harvest in late summer to early fall as the hulls split.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This popular self-fertile, semi-dwarf almond is ideal for home gardens where only one tree fits. Plant in a warm, well-drained spot and prune to keep it in an open vase shape. Soft-shelled, sweet nuts ripen in late summer when the hulls dry and split.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This late-blooming almond escapes many spring frosts, making it the most reliable choice for cooler regions. Its showy pink blossoms are followed by hard-shelled nuts, and a second tree improves the crop. Harvest the nuts in early fall once the fuzzy hulls open.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: A major commercial variety, 'Carmel' bears heavily and pollinates well with 'Nonpareil'. Give it full sun, well-drained soil, and protection from late frosts during its early bloom. The soft-shelled kernels are harvested in late summer as hulls split.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring.

directions: This productive California cultivar yields small, plump kernels and is often grown alongside 'Padre' as a pollination pair. Plant in a hot, well-drained location and prune yearly to maintain light penetration. Nuts are ready in early fall when the hulls dry and open.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: This is the standard female orchard cultivar, producing large, high-quality nuts on grafted trees. It must be paired with a compatible male such as 'Peters' whose bloom overlaps for pollination. Harvest in early autumn when hulls slip easily and shells have split.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: This is the classic male cultivar grown solely to pollinate 'Kerman' and other females; it bears no nuts itself. Plant one among every eight to ten female trees, positioned so prevailing winds carry pollen across the orchard. Its bloom is timed to overlap the females for reliable nut set.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in early spring.

directions: A newer female cultivar, 'Golden Hills' ripens earlier than 'Kerman' and produces well-filled, easily split nuts. Pair it with a compatible early-blooming male pollinizer for a full crop. Harvest in early fall when the hulls loosen and shells have opened.

planting.note: Set out dormant bare-root or potted trees in early spring.

directions: Plant the graft union two to three inches above the soil and stake the young trunk through the first season. Thin clusters to a single fruit per spur once the June drop passes to build size and crispness. Pick when the skin flushes mottled red over yellow and the flesh shatters juicily underfoot in early autumn.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Dig a wide hole and spread the roots without burying the graft, then firm and water in. Provide a second apple variety nearby for pollination and thin heavy sets so fruit does not stay small. Harvest the striped orange-red apples in late summer once the ground color yellows and they snap free with a gentle upward twist.

planting.note: Set out dormant trees in spring where the season is long enough to ripen late fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union clear of the soil and mulch the root zone to hold moisture. This is a very late variety, so leave the fruit hanging into mid or late autumn to develop its dense sweetness. Pick when the pink-red blush deepens and the sugars build; the apples store for many months in a cold cellar.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring before growth begins.

directions: Firm the soil around the roots and keep the graft above grade, watering deeply the first summer. Prune to an open center so the deep red, elongated fruit colors evenly in the light. Gather the apples in early to mid autumn when the classic five-lobed base is fully developed and the skin glows dark crimson.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; this reliable pollinator suits most orchards.

directions: Set the tree at the same depth it grew in the nursery and keep the union exposed. It self-thins somewhat but hand-thinning improves fruit size and prevents biennial bearing. Harvest the pale yellow-green apples in early autumn when a faint golden warmth spreads across the skin and the flesh turns sweet and mellow.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site with a long growing season.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile soil and stake the leader while it establishes. Thin the vigorous crop early so the tree does not fall into alternate-year bearing. Leave the red-over-green apples to hang until mid or late autumn, then pick when their sharp-sweet flavor is fully developed for long keeping.

planting.note: Plant in spring alongside two other varieties, since this triploid needs pollen.

directions: Because this variety produces sterile pollen, plant at least two other compatible apples nearby to set a crop. Keep the union above the soil and thin the large fruit to reduce limb breakage. Harvest the big red-blushed golden apples in mid autumn when the honeyed, slightly tart flesh reaches full flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a dependable McIntosh-Delicious cross for the North.

directions: Plant in full sun and spread the roots gently before backfilling with native soil. This variety resists bruising and holds its fruit well, so routine thinning is mainly for size. Pick the dark red apples in early autumn when the crisp white flesh turns sweet with a mild tang.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a cold-hardy classic well suited to northern gardens.

directions: Set the tree with the graft clear of the soil and water it in thoroughly. This variety drops its fruit readily when ripe, so check the crop often and pick promptly. Gather the red-and-green apples in early autumn when the tender, aromatic flesh gives its signature perfumed sweetness.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this club apple is a Gala and Braeburn cross with dense flesh.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and water deeply through the first two seasons to establish it. Thin the fruit to spread the load and prevent overcropping on this heavy bearer. Harvest the firm red-over-green apples in late autumn when the crunch is hard and the flavor turns sharply sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring only where the season is long and warm enough to ripen late fruit.

directions: Give this very late variety a warm wall or the sunniest corner so it finishes its long ripening. Keep the union above soil and thin early to steady the crop. Leave the pink-blushed apples hanging into late autumn and pick once the effervescent, tangy-sweet flavor and rosy skin fully develop.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a modern Braeburn and Gala club cross with slow-browning flesh.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained ground and keep the graft clear of the soil. Thin heavy sets so the large, round fruit sizes up evenly. Harvest the deep red apples in mid to late autumn when the sweet, mild flesh stays white and crisp after cutting.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; a low-acid Canadian chance seedling.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and firm the soil gently over the spread roots. Its flesh browns slowly and it bruises little, making it forgiving to handle. Pick the pink-and-yellow apples in mid autumn when the honeyed, low-acid sweetness with a floral note peaks.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a heritage American dessert apple with bright acidity.

directions: Plant in full sun with the graft above grade and mulch to conserve moisture. Prune for good airflow, as the variety can be prone to mildew in crowded canopies. Pick the deep red apples in early to mid autumn when the tender flesh delivers its lively spicy-tart flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; a hardy McIntosh-type from British Columbia.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and stake the trunk while it roots in. This reliable, self-thinning variety needs only light hand-thinning of dense clusters. Pick the deep maroon apples in early to mid autumn when the crisp white flesh is sweet with a hint of wine.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a productive German dessert apple sold also as Pinata.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil with the graft union above ground. It bears heavily and early, so thin diligently to keep fruit size up and avoid biennial cropping. Harvest the orange-red striped apples in mid autumn when the aromatic, tangy-sweet flesh is firm and juicy.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Gala and Braeburn club cross with balanced flavor.

directions: Plant in full sun on free-draining ground and keep the union exposed. Thin the crop so the bicolored fruit develops full size and even color. Pick the red-blushed yellow apples in mid to late autumn when the crisp flesh balances sweetness with a refreshing tang.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Golden Delicious and Topaz cross with non-browning flesh.

directions: Set the tree in full sun with the graft above the soil and water in well. This scab-resistant variety needs little spraying and thins to a single fruit per cluster easily. Harvest the bright yellow apples in mid autumn when the crunchy flesh turns sweet with a floral, slightly tangy edge.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; a Honeycrisp and Zestar club cross.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft union a few inches above grade. Thin clusters to one fruit to encourage the large size and explosive crunch it inherited from Honeycrisp. Pick the red-striped apples in early autumn when the coarse, breaking flesh mixes bright sweetness with a spiced tang.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Honeycrisp and Enterprise cross bred in Washington.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil and keep the union clear of the ground. Thin heavy sets so the large, firm fruit develops fully on this productive variety. Harvest the deep red, lightly speckled apples in mid to late autumn when the exceptionally crisp flesh balances sweet and tart and stores for months.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a miniature snacking club apple bred in New Zealand.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and keep the graft above the soil line. Unlike most varieties, do not over-thin, since the small fruit size is a feature of this snack apple. Pick the little red-blushed apples in early to mid autumn when the crisp flesh turns sweet and juicy.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in spring; a Washington chance seedling with striped skin.

directions: Plant in full sun and firm the soil over the roots without burying the union. Thin the crop to size up the fruit and steady annual bearing. Harvest the red-striped apples in mid to late autumn when the dense, non-browning flesh turns sweet-tart and crisp.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a very cold-hardy early apple from Minnesota.

directions: Plant in full sun in cold-winter regions and keep the union above grade. This early bearer sets heavily, so thin clusters to build fruit size. Pick the red-over-cream apples in late summer when the crisp, coarse flesh gives a bright sweet-tart snap with a hint of brown sugar.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Golden Delicious and Ingrid Marie cross from the Netherlands.

directions: Plant in full sun with the graft clear of the soil and stake while young. It crops early and heavily, so thin well to avoid small fruit and alternate bearing. Harvest the red-flushed yellow apples in early to mid autumn when the honeyed, tangy flavor is fully developed.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Golden Delicious and Cox cross prized for flavor.

directions: Plant in a sunny, sheltered spot with the union above the soil. This flavorful but light cropper benefits from good feeding and minimal thinning. Harvest the small red-striped apples in mid autumn when the aromatic, honeyed flesh reaches its rich Cox-like flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early English dessert apple best eaten fresh off the tree.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union above the soil and water through dry spells. It bears early in life and resists scab, needing little intervention. Pick the bright red apples in late summer and eat them soon, as this variety does not store well.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Scottish dessert apple and excellent pollinator.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union above grade, tolerant even of cooler, wetter sites. It flowers reliably and pollinates many other apples, so it earns a place in mixed plantings. Pick the yellow apples flushed with red stripes in early autumn when the juicy flesh turns brisk and refreshing; use them soon, as they bruise easily.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Cox-style English dessert apple that can bear biennially.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft union exposed above the soil. Thin firmly and evenly each year to counter its tendency toward alternate cropping. Harvest the purple-red flushed apples in mid autumn when the aromatic, Cox-like flesh is sweet and mellow.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an easy, compact Cox seedling for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union above soil; it thrives even where Cox itself struggles. This tidy, disease-tolerant tree crops reliably with little fuss. Pick the orange-flushed golden apples in mid autumn when the crisp flesh takes on its aromatic, nutty sweetness.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Cox and Idared cross also sold as Red Pippin.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft above grade in well-drained soil. It crops heavily and regularly, so thin the fruit to maintain size. Harvest the red-striped apples in mid autumn when the firm flesh delivers a Cox-like aromatic sweetness that also keeps well.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Worcester Pearmain and James Grieve cross from Sweden.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union above the soil and stake while establishing. This hardy, reliable cropper makes a good pollinator and needs little care. Pick the bright red apples in early autumn when the crisp, juicy flesh turns brisk and refreshing; use them promptly.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early yellow chance seedling from Virginia.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft union clear of the soil. As one of the earliest yellow apples, thin the crop to size the fruit before its short season closes. Pick the pale green-gold apples in late summer when the crisp, mild flesh browns slowly after slicing.

planting.note: Plant in spring with pollinators, as this large triploid sheds sterile pollen.

directions: Plant two other compatible varieties nearby to fruit this vigorous triploid. Give it room to spread and keep the union above soil. Harvest the large green-gold apples in mid to late autumn when the coarse, honeyed flesh turns sweet with a mild tang.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a McIntosh-type dessert apple prized in the Northeast.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft union above grade in well-drained soil. It tends to bear biennially and drops ripe fruit, so thin firmly and pick promptly. Harvest the dark red-purple apples in early to mid autumn when the tender, aromatic flesh is at its sweet, wine-like peak.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a highly disease-resistant apple for low-spray orchards.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union above the soil; its strong scab resistance suits organic growing. It sets heavily, so thin clusters to size the fruit and steady annual bearing. Pick the red-over-yellow apples in mid autumn when the crisp, juicy flesh turns sweet-tart and lively.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a late, disease-resistant apple that stores well.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the union clear of the soil; it resists scab and fire blight well. Thin the crop to build fruit size on this productive tree. Harvest the glossy dark red apples in late autumn, and let them rest a few weeks in storage as the spicy-tart flavor mellows and sweetens.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the season is long enough for this very late apple.

directions: Plant in full sun with the graft above grade and give it a long growing season to finish. This scab-resistant keeper holds on the tree late, so leave it until the yellow ground color deepens. Harvest the firm golden apples in late autumn; the tart flavor sweetens into a rich, complex balance after a few weeks of storage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the earliest disease-resistant yellow apples.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the union above the soil; its scab resistance eases care. As a very early bearer, thin promptly to size the fruit before the short window ends. Pick the bright yellow apples in mid to late summer when the crisp, tangy-sweet flesh is fresh and juicy.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early, disease-resistant red apple with good flavor.

directions: Plant in full sun with the graft union above grade in fertile, well-drained soil. Its strong disease resistance makes it easy to grow with minimal spraying. Pick the dark red apples in late summer when the crisp flesh delivers a rich, spicy-sweet flavor unusual for an early variety.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a sweet, deeply striped Fuji sport grown as a club apple.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil and keep the union above grade. As a Fuji type, it needs a long, warm season to develop full sweetness. Harvest the crimson-striped apples in mid to late autumn when the very sweet, juicy flesh is firm and crisp.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a New Zealand club apple blending several parent varieties.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft clear of the soil, watering well while it establishes. Thin the crop to develop the round, evenly colored fruit. Pick the red-blushed apples in mid autumn when the crisp flesh balances sweetness with a clean, refreshing tang.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Swiss club apple with Idared, Maigold and Elstar parentage.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union above grade in fertile, well-drained soil. Thin the fruit to maintain size on this productive variety. Harvest the red-striped apples in mid to late autumn when the firm, juicy flesh turns crisp with a lively sweet-tart flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Fuji and Braeburn club apple with a spiced note.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft union clear of the soil. Thin heavy sets so the firm fruit develops full size and color. Harvest the red-blushed apples in mid to late autumn when the dense, sweet flesh shows its subtle cinnamon-like warmth.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Gala and Red Delicious cross from New Zealand.

directions: Plant in full sun with the graft above grade and stake while young. Thin the crop to size the tall, elongated fruit inherited from its Delicious parent. Pick the red-striped golden apples in mid autumn when the crisp, low-acid flesh turns very sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a disease-resistant Czech apple with a sharp, aromatic bite.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the union above the soil; its scab resistance eases low-spray growing. Thin the crop to build size and steady annual bearing. Harvest the red-striped orange apples in mid autumn when the firm flesh gives its bright, tangy-sweet flavor, and store them to mellow.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a cold-hardy Golden Delicious type from Minnesota.

directions: Plant in full sun in cold regions with the graft union above grade. Bred to bring Golden Delicious flavor to harsh winters, it needs a pollinator nearby. Harvest the yellow-gold apples in mid autumn when the crisp, mild flesh turns sweet and honeyed.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a disease-resistant dual dessert apple, a good pollinator for Liberty.

directions: Plant in full sun with the union above the soil; its scab resistance suits organic care. It bears heavily, so thin clusters to keep the fruit sizing up. Harvest the red apples in mid autumn when the crisp, juicy flesh turns sweet-tart and ready for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a cold-hardy McIntosh cross with slow-browning white flesh.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the graft union above grade, staking while young. This hardy, reliable variety resists browning once cut, making it good for slicing. Harvest the red-over-green apples in early to mid autumn when the tender flesh turns sweet with a mild tartness.

planting.note: Set out dormant bare-root trees in early spring while the ground is workable.

directions: Plant the graft union two to three inches above the finished soil line and stake young trees in windy sites. Rome Beauty is a classic firm baking apple that holds its shape when cooked, so let fruit color deeply on the tree before a mid-to-late autumn pick. Thin heavy clusters in early summer to size up the crop and reduce alternate bearing.

planting.note: Set out bare-root stock in early spring on a site with good air drainage.

directions: This lopsided, firm apple was long the backbone of the Appalachian processing trade and bakes and cans beautifully. Harvest the yellow-green fruit blushed with red late in the season, as it hangs well and improves after a few weeks in storage. Provide a mid-season pollenizer and thin fruit to keep individual size up.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; one of the earliest apples to ripen.

directions: This pale, translucent-skinned apple ripens in early to midsummer, well ahead of most varieties. The soft, tart flesh cooks down almost instantly into a smooth applesauce, so gather it as soon as the skin turns from green to waxy yellow. Use quickly, as the fruit bruises and softens within days of picking.

planting.note: Set out in early spring; an improved, larger successor to Yellow Transparent.

directions: Lodi yields large, pale green early apples that are tart and best for sauce and pies rather than fresh eating. Pick in early summer while still firm and slightly underripe for the sharpest cooking flavor. Thin developing fruit to reduce a tendency toward biennial bearing.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a very hardy old French-Canadian variety.

directions: Named the Snow Apple for its brilliant white, sometimes red-streaked flesh under crimson skin. Pick in early to mid-autumn and enjoy fresh, as the aromatic fruit is at its best soon after harvest. It is thought to be a parent of McIntosh and shares that cold hardiness.

subcategory: pome; heritage drying and sauce apple

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; an old late-summer American apple.

directions: The pale yellow fruit carries a delicate pink cheek where the sun strikes it. Harvest in late summer for drying, sauce, and cider, as the tart flesh slices and dries cleanly. Pick before fully soft, since it is best used promptly rather than stored.

subcategory: pome; heritage dessert and cider apple

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a self-fertile West Virginia heirloom.

directions: A reputed parent of Golden Delicious, this spicy golden apple is richly flavored and high in sugar. It is largely self-fertile, so a single tree can still fruit, though a pollenizer improves the crop. Harvest in mid-autumn for fresh eating or press it into a distinctive sweet cider.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; adapted to warm southern orchards.

directions: This tough, handsome red apple was once a shipping mainstay thanks to its remarkable keeping ability. The flesh is firm and mild, best for cooking and long winter storage rather than dessert. It tolerates heat and travels well, making it a durable choice for warm-climate gardens.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; bred in Minnesota for severe winters.

directions: Haralson is a firm, tart, complex apple bred for the upper Midwest and remarkably winter-hardy. Harvest in mid-autumn for fresh eating, pies, and sauce, and enjoy its good storage life through early winter. Provide another hardy apple for cross-pollination.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy, disease-tolerant Minnesota release.

directions: This hardy University of Minnesota apple is known for a distinctive sweet flavor with hints of cherry candy or vanilla. It resists scab and shrugs off hard winters, making it low-fuss in cold regions. Harvest the red-striped fruit in early to mid-autumn and eat fresh at peak crispness.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy early apple from Minnesota.

directions: Zestar delivers crisp, juicy, sweet-tart fruit remarkably early for such a cold-hardy variety. Pick the red-blushed apples in late summer to early fall and enjoy their unusual staying crispness for an early apple. It stores far better than most early-season types.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy Minnesota apple and a Honeycrisp parent.

directions: Keepsake is a hard, sugary, aromatic apple that ripens late and lives up to its name in storage. One of the parents of Honeycrisp, it keeps its crunch for months in a cool cellar. Harvest in mid-to-late autumn and let the flavor deepen over several weeks of storage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a large, hardy Minnesota dessert apple.

directions: Fireside bears large, red, mild-sweet apples on a vigorous, very hardy tree. Harvest in mid-to-late autumn for fresh eating and enjoy its good winter storage life. Its close relative Connell Red is a redder sport of the same apple.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy, late-keeping Minnesota apple.

directions: Prairie Spy is a firm, balanced sweet-tart apple whose flavor actually improves after a few months in storage. Harvest the large red-striped fruit in mid-to-late autumn and hold it cool through winter. The hardy tree tends toward biennial bearing, so thin fruit in heavy years.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy early apple for northern gardens.

directions: State Fair ripens in late summer, right around fair season, with balanced sweet-tart red fruit. It is one of the better early apples for fresh eating and light cooking. Use promptly after picking, since like most early varieties it does not store long.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a scab-immune summer apple from the PRI program.

directions: This deep purple-red summer apple resists apple scab, making it excellent for low-spray orchards. The rich, spicy fruit ripens over a couple of weeks in mid-to-late summer, so plan to pick more than once. Eat fresh soon after harvest, as it is an early apple with limited storage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a scab-resistant summer variety.

directions: Redfree produces glossy, bright red, mild-sweet apples that resist scab and several other diseases. Harvest in late summer and enjoy the crisp, juicy fruit fresh, using it within a few weeks. Its disease resistance makes it a good low-input choice for humid climates.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a Jonathan-Worcester cross from Japan.

directions: Akane offers bright red, tart-sweet fruit with clean, crisp white flesh early in the season. It resists scab and mildew reasonably well and holds up nicely in pies. Pick in late summer to early fall and use within a few weeks for best flavor.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a low-chill apple suited to mild-winter zones.

directions: This pale yellow apple with a pink blush carries a faintly banana-like aroma and needs only modest winter chill. Its relatively low chilling requirement suits warmer regions where many apples fail. Harvest in mid-autumn for fresh eating and use it as a widely compatible pollenizer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a triploid Dutch Cox-type needing pollenizers.

directions: A Dutch descendant of Cox's Orange Pippin, this apple is intensely aromatic, sharp, and high in both sugar and acid. Because it is triploid it needs two other apples blooming nearby for pollination. Harvest in mid-autumn once the rich, complex flavor fully develops.

common_name: Reine des Reinettes (King of the Pippins)

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a classic European dual-purpose apple.

directions: Known in Britain as King of the Pippins, this golden, red-flushed apple is aromatic and firm, good for both dessert and cooking. Harvest in mid-autumn and let the rich, nutty flavor develop in short storage. The reliable tree crops heavily and suits traditional orchards.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a triploid Dutch culinary apple needing pollenizers.

directions: This large, russeted, tart Dutch apple is a superb keeper and a favorite for baking and sauce. As a triploid it contributes no pollen, so plant two compatible pollenizers alongside it. Harvest in mid-autumn and store cool, where the sharp flavor mellows over winter.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a conical Victorian English dessert apple.

directions: This conical, partly russeted English apple has a dry, rich, nutty flavor prized by connoisseurs. Harvest in mid-autumn and store cool, where it keeps well into winter and the flavor concentrates. The tree tolerates less-than-ideal sites and still crops reliably.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy, adaptable English heirloom.

directions: Claygate Pearmain is a greenish, russet-flecked dessert apple with a rich, aromatic, nutty taste. It is hardy and adaptable, tolerating cooler and shadier gardens than many dessert varieties. Harvest in mid-autumn and hold in cool storage into early winter.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a handsome, adaptable English dual-purpose apple.

directions: Charles Ross produces large, showy, red-flushed golden apples good for both eating fresh and cooking. It tolerates cold and chalky soils and crops well without fuss. Harvest in early to mid-autumn and use within a couple of months, as it does not store long.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a heavy-cropping large cooking apple.

directions: This English cooker grows to a very large size and has produced record-setting show apples. The flesh keeps some shape when cooked, making it useful for both sauce and baking. Harvest in mid-autumn and store cool, where large fruit keeps for a couple of months.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; one of the earliest-ripening English apples.

directions: This little flushed-and-striped apple is one of the first to ripen, offering a sharp, refreshing bite in high summer. Pick the fruit as it colors, ideally letting it fall or come away with the lightest touch, and eat it within a day or two. It does not keep at all, so enjoy it straight from the tree.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a reliable, well-flavored English dessert apple.

directions: Lord Lambourne is a crisp, juicy, sweet-sharp apple with striped red skin and dependable cropping. It flowers reliably and sets fruit well with a suitable pollenizer nearby. Harvest in early to mid-autumn and use within a month or so of picking.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a New Zealand Cox-Delicious cross and Gala parent.

directions: A cross of Cox's Orange Pippin and Red Delicious, this aromatic apple is a parent of Gala. The orange-red fruit is sweet, richly perfumed, and firm. Harvest in mid-autumn and enjoy fresh, storing a portion cool for several weeks.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a hardy Cox seedling with an aniseed note.

directions: Ellison's Orange is a Cox seedling with a striking, some say aniseed-like, aromatic flavor. It is hardy and crops reliably even in cooler districts. Pick the red-flushed fruit in early autumn and eat fresh promptly, as it soon goes soft.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a well-flavored late-keeping English russet.

directions: Rosemary Russet is a sharp, aromatic, partly russeted apple with a fine balance of sugar and acid. Harvest in mid-autumn and store cool, where the flavor sharpens and the fruit keeps into winter. It suits both the dessert plate and a rich cider blend.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a Cornell club apple marketed as SnapDragon.

directions: SnapDragon is a Cornell-bred Honeycrisp descendant sold as the trademarked selection NY 1, offering a monster crunch and sweet, spiced flavor. Harvest in mid-autumn and enjoy the crisp, juicy fruit fresh. It stores well and holds its texture for weeks in cool conditions.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a late club apple marketed as EverCrisp.

directions: A Honeycrisp-Fuji cross sold as the trademarked MAIA-1, EverCrisp combines a dense crunch with sweet, mild flavor and outstanding storage. Harvest late, in mid-to-late autumn, once the fruit fully colors and sugars up. It keeps its crispness in cool storage for many months.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a managed club apple sold as SugarBee.

directions: SugarBee arose as an open-pollinated Honeycrisp seedling and is sold under license as the selection CN121, known for high sugar and a honeyed, crisp bite. Harvest in mid-to-late autumn once the fruit reaches full sweetness. It eats best fresh and stores well when kept cold.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a New Zealand club apple marketed as Koru.

directions: Koru is a chance seedling with Fuji parentage, sold under license as the variety Plumac, offering sweet, honeyed flavor with a hint of citrus and a firm crunch. Harvest in mid-to-late autumn when fully colored. The dense flesh resists browning when cut and stores well.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a late-ripening managed apple from Washington.

directions: Discovered as a chance seedling in Washington, Lady Alice is a very late, sweet-tart apple with dense flesh that resists browning. Harvest late in autumn once it fully ripens. It stores exceptionally well, keeping its crispness for months in cool conditions.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; very hardy in northern zones.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained ground and provide a pollination partner. This old Quebec variety is named for its snow-white, aromatic flesh under crimson skin. Harvest the small dessert apples in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a small edible and cider crab.

directions: Plant in full sun where its heavy bloom can serve as an orchard pollinator. Wickson produces tiny, intensely sweet-tart crabapples prized for cider and fresh nibbling. Harvest the cherry-sized red-and-yellow fruit in early autumn once sugars peak.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; extremely cold-hardy edible crab.

directions: Set out in full sun in a spot with good air movement. This University of Minnesota selection bears larger-than-typical crabapples with a nutty, sweet flavor for fresh eating. Pick the bronze-blushed fruit in late summer to early fall.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a hardy Canadian edible crab.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil in even the coldest regions. Kerr yields deep-red, plum-sized fruit that sweetens after frost and is excellent for jelly and cider. Harvest in fall, leaving some on the tree to mellow with cold nights.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a very hardy jelly and pollinator crab.

directions: Plant in full sun where its abundant white bloom can pollinate nearby apples. Dolgo bears elongated, bright-red crabapples that are high in pectin and ideal for ruby jelly. Harvest in late summer when the fruit colors fully and softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a large sweet crab for eating and pickling.

directions: Set out in full sun and keep evenly watered through establishment. Whitney produces unusually large, sweet crabapples good for fresh eating, spiced pickles, and cider. Pick the glossy red-striped fruit in late summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a sweet dessert crab.

directions: Plant in full sun on fertile, well-drained ground. This Minnesota edible crab bears sweet, aromatic red-and-orange fruit good enough to eat out of hand. Harvest in late summer to early fall when the color deepens.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; among the hardiest edible crabs.

directions: Choose a full-sun site; this prairie-bred crab thrives where few apples survive. Trailman offers yellow fruit with a red blush that is sweet enough for fresh eating and good for jelly. Harvest in late summer before the first hard freeze.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a classic American cider crab.

directions: Plant in full sun; this vigorous, disease-tough crab was a colonial cider staple. Its small, astringent fruit yields juice that ferments into well-structured hard cider. Harvest the little red-and-green apples in autumn and press promptly.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a red-fleshed cider and cooking apple.

directions: Plant in full sun on well-drained soil and allow room for a full-sized canopy. Redfield carries crabapple parentage and bears tart apples with striking red flesh for rosy cider and sauce. Harvest in mid-autumn once the skin turns deep purple-red.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; an early Japanese-New Zealand dessert apple.

directions: Plant in full sun and thin fruit clusters to size up the crop. A Gala and Akane cross, Sansa is a sweet, crisp early apple with reliable flavor. Harvest the red-flushed fruit in late summer before it drops.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a long-keeping dual-purpose apple.

directions: Plant in full sun and thin the heavy set for larger fruit. Idared stores exceptionally well, often improving in flavor after a month in the cellar. Harvest the bright-red, firm apples in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a German late dessert apple.

directions: Plant in full sun and prune yearly to admit light into the canopy. Gloster produces tall, dark-red, firm apples with a crisp, mildly tart flavor and good storage. Harvest in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; an ancient small French dessert apple.

directions: Plant in full sun and thin lightly since fruit is naturally small. This centuries-old French variety bears tiny, flat, red-cheeked apples once used for holiday decoration. Harvest in mid to late autumn; the fruit keeps very well.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a scab-immune dessert apple.

directions: Plant in full sun; its resistance to scab and mildew reduces spraying. Priscilla bears firm, red-blushed apples with a mild, slightly spicy flavor and good storage. Harvest in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a scab-resistant, long-keeping apple.

directions: Plant in full sun and thin fruit for best size on this productive tree. Crimson Crisp offers deep-red, very crisp, tart-sweet apples that store for months. Harvest in mid to late autumn once the color deepens fully.
